design_philosophy.core_loop: Alternating between god-like city-building simulation (overhead view) where player guides civilization growth via miracles and monster lair sealing, and action platformer sequences where player controls an animated statue to defeat bosses and accrue score
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balancing civilization development and population growth (which increases player power via leveling) against direct action combat challenges; overcoming evil lieutenants to regain lost divine power
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Playing as a godlike being rebuilding civilizations after defeat, performing miracles, and progressively reclaiming power through faith and belief of the people
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Unique hybrid genre blend rejecting pure platformer convention; deeply opinionated religious allegory (God vs Satan sanitized to Master vs Tanzra for Western release) exploring themes of faith, civilization, and divine relevance; ending subverts power fantasy by suggesting people outgrow need for deity once empowered

difficulty.checkpointing: Action sequences separated into two per area with boss encounters at end of each
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player power scales with civilization population (leveling grants HP and SP for miracles); progression in simulation mode directly impacts action sequence capability; North American version made action segments easier with reduced spell costs

progression.upgrade_system: Master levels up via total population growth, increasing hit points and SP (magic points) for miracle casting
progression.unlock_mechanism: Defeating Tanzra's six lieutenants in sequence unlocks progression toward final stronghold Death Heim; sealing four monster lairs per region enables advanced structure building and population capacity increases
replayability_hooks: Score accumulation during action sequences incentivizes replay
Multiple miracle types (lightning, rain, sunlight, wind, earthquakes) offer varied civilization management approaches

world_building_techniques: Instruction booklet framing of Master's backstory and Tanzra's division of world into six lands
Environmental storytelling via discovered temples becoming empty at game's end, signifying loss of faith
Boss design rooted in real-world mythology (Greek mythology, Hinduism) reinforcing religious allegory
Narrative arc of divine irrelevance: Master loses powers through lack of belief, regains them through rebuilding civilization, ultimately departs when people achieve self-sufficiency

boss_design.philosophy: Bosses represent Tanzra's six lieutenants and embody mythological/religious iconography (e.g., manticore in Bloodpool); defeating them is narrative progression mechanism tied to Master's power recovery; final level is boss marathon culminating in Tanzra himself

faction_indicators: Civilization population levels indicate faith/belief in Master
Monster lairs represent corruption/evil influence requiring community action to seal
Advanced structure availability tied to civilization level suggests societal development stages
danger_signals: Flying monsters spawning from lairs threaten civilization expansion
Four unsealed monster lairs per region indicate active corruption
Tanzra and six lieutenants as existential evil threatening world order
environmental_storytelling: Empty temples at game's end visually communicate civilization's spiritual evolution
Region-specific architecture and monster types reflect regional corruption by different lieutenants
Master's sky palace as unreachable sanctuary reflecting divine transcendence
